data.title: JPMorgan CEO Dimon says U.S. stock market could be headed for serious correction-report
data.description:
The U.S. stock market could be at high risk of a serious correction within the next six months to two years, according to JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon. 

Dimon, in an interview with BBC, added that the U.S. has become a "less reliable" partner on the global platform and that he was still a little worried about the inflation in the country. 

However, Dimon emphasized that the Federal Reserve should remain independent. This comes amid President Donald Trump's strong criticism towards Fed Chair Jerome Powell and concerns about the autonomy of the central bank. 

The bank's chief believes that there are a lot of things that are leading to an environment of uncertainty, including geopolitical risks, fiscal spending and the remilitarization of the world.

"All these things cause a lot of issues that we don't know how to answer. So I say the level of uncertainty should be higher in most people's minds than what I would call normal," he said in the interview. 

Dimon held an overall positive view on artificial intelligence, one of the main drivers of the stock market rally in recent times. "The way I look at it is AI is real, AI in total will pay off," he said. 

Since the start of the year, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 14.8%, Dow (DJI) has risen 9.5%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has risen 19.3%. 

Stock index futures were almost flat on Thursday, as a tech rally on Wednesday pushed the Nasdaq Composite to another record high, while investors looked forward to Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's welcome remarks at the Community Bank Conference. 

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the S&P 500: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Ørsted to cut 25% of jobs amid strategic shift in renewable energy
data.description:
Danish renewable energy giant Ørsted (OTCPK:DNNGY) (OTCPK:DOGEF) plans to reduce its global workforce by roughly a quarter as it narrows its focus to core offshore and European markets.

The company said about 2,000 roles will be eliminated by 2027, trimming its current headcount of around 8,000. The first round of reductions -- roughly 500 jobs, including 235 in Denmark -- will take place before the end of this year.

Chief Executive Rasmus Errboe said the downsizing reflects both the completion of several major projects and a sharpened market strategy.

“We want to create a more efficient and flexible organisation and a more competitive Ørsted, ready to bid on new value-accretive offshore wind projects,” he said in a statement.

Errboe added that cutting costs across development, construction and operations is critical to keeping Ørsted competitive in offshore wind.

The job cuts will be achieved through attrition, divestments, outsourcing and layoffs.

The announcement follows Ørsted’s $9.4 billion equity raise earlier this week, intended to support ongoing offshore wind construction at a time when the industry is contending with rising costs and financial pressure.

data.title: Assurant acquires OptoFidelity’s mobile device testing solutions
data.description:
• Assurant (NYSE:AIZ) said on Thursday that it has acquired the portfolio of mobile device test automation technology from OptoFidelity, a provider of advanced optical metrology systems for mobile devices. 
  • OptoFidelity’s technology improves operational efficiency and further strengthens Assurant’s ability to scale, optimize resources, and easily integrate with existing processes to support future innovation. 
  • Founded in 2005, OptoFidelity produces advanced mobile device testing systems known for precision, repeatability, and traceability. 
  • In the 20 years since its founding, the company has delivered over 9,000 test systems to mobile carriers, manufacturers, and retailers worldwide.

data.title: Neogen to cut 10% of workforce; shares rise
data.description:
• Neogen (NASDAQ:NEOG) on Thursday said it would cut 10% of its workforce to reduce costs after reaffirming its full-year outlook, a move that lifted shares.
  • Targeted operational improvements to gross margin, focusing on core process execution to drive improvement in inventory, Neogen (NASDAQ:NEOG) said.
  • The update comes after the company reaffirmed its full-year outlook for fiscal year 2026.
  • Neogen (NEOG) +10% at $6.40 in premarket trading.

data.title: CDT Equity announces 1-for-8 reverse stock split effective October 10
data.description:
• CDT Equity (NASDAQ:CDT) to implement a 1-for-8 reverse stock split effective October 10, 2025.
  • Trading on a split-adjusted basis will begin on October 13, 2025.
  • Purpose of the split: potential to raise more funds to support the company’s strategy.
  • Outstanding shares will be reduced to ~1.35M post-split.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Delta Air Lines eyes strong year-end fueling airline sector gains
data.description:
Shares of Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) are trading higher into Thursday’s open, taking the airline sector higher in tandem, as the carrier’s third quarter results coupled with an upbeat outlook for the remainder of the year underscored Delta’s (NYSE:DAL) success in navigating a turbulent start to the year.

"Momentum is continuing into the final stretch of our Centennial year, positioning us to deliver strong December quarter earnings.  Looking to 2026, Delta is well positioned to deliver top-line growth, margin expansion and earnings improvement consistent with our long-term financial framework,” said Delta CEO Ed Bastian.

In contrast to the first quarter of this year, in which the carrier pulled its guidance due to the uneven economic environment, Delta (NYSE:DAL) now expects to report a profit of $1.60 to $1.90 per share in the fourth quarter on a 2% to 4% increase in revenue, exceeding expectations for a profit of $1.64 per share on 1.24% revenue growth.

For the full year, Delta (DAL) now expects profits to be at the higher end of its previous range, setting a goal of ~$6 per share versus a range of $5.25 to $6.25 previously. This beats the consensus estimate of $5.78 per share.

Led by strength in its premium, corporate, and loyalty segments, Q3 adjusted revenue grew 4.2% to $15.2B, contributing to an adjusted profit of $1.71 per share, both of which exceeded expectations.

Operationally, the carrier showed modest improvement from a year ago, as well, with revenue passenger miles up 2%, available seat miles up 4%, and total revenue per available seat mile (TRASM) increasing by 2%, all of which helped offset a 1% increase in the cost per available seat mile (CASM). Excluding fuel, CASM was roughly unchanged at $0.135.

DAL last traded 6% higher into the open, giving a boost to shares of United Airlines (UAL), American Airlines (UAL), Southwest Airlines (LUV), JetBlue (JBLU), and Alaska Air Group (ALK). The Global Jets ETF (JETS) is up 2%.

data.title: Byrna Technologies rises after reporting Q3 results
data.description:
• Byrna Technologies press release (NASDAQ:BYRN): Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.09 beats by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $28.18M (+35.2% Y/Y) in-line.
  • The year-over-year growth was largely driven by strong dealer and chain store sales tied to Byrna’s expanding retail presence, the success of new marketing initiatives, and broader brand adoption. Web traffic began to build late in the quarter and has continued into the fourth fiscal quarter of 2025 (Q4 2025), supporting stronger e-commerce activity.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TLRY, AKRO, DAL, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched higher in premarket trading on Thursday, following a strong tech rally that pushed the Nasdaq to a new record high in the previous session.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Richardson Electronics (NASDAQ:RELL) +22% - Shares jumped higher after the company reported better-than-expected FQ1 results, driven by strong growth in its semiconductor wafer fab business. Excluding the divested Healthcare unit, net sales rose by 6.8% Y/Y. The company's focus on a more profitable sales mix and disciplined cost control was highly effective, leading its operating income to more than triple compared to the prior year. CEO Edward J. Richardson noted the solid results reflect the company's value proposition and market diversity, adding that they generated positive operating cash flow for the sixth consecutive quarter and are confident in delivering improved performance for the entirety of FY2026.
  • Akero Therapeutics (NASDAQ:AKRO) +19% - Shares advanced after the company announced a definitive agreement to be acquired by Novo Nordisk (NVO) for up to $5.2B in cash. Under the agreement, Akero shareholders will get $54.00 in cash for each share when the deal closes, plus a $6.00 payment that will be made if efruxifermin gets full U.S. regulatory approval for MASH-related compensated cirrhosis The upfront cash portion values Akero at $4.7B, representing a 42% premium to its May 19, 2025, closing price. The deal, unanimously approved by Akero’s board, is expected to close by year-end.
  • Tilray Brands (NASDAQ:TLRY) +13% - Shares surged after the Canadian cannabis company posted its first revenue beat in four quarters and raised its full-year outlook. Q1 revenue rose ~5% Y/Y to $209.5M, topping estimates by ~$3.8M, driven by a 9% rise in distribution sales to $74M, while cannabis revenue grew ~5% to $64.5M and beverages were flat at $55.7M. Operating costs fell ~43% to $55.4M amid lower amortization and non-cash expenses, helping Tilray swing to $1.5M net income from a $34.7M loss a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA rose ~9% to $10.2M, and adjusted net income improved to $3.9M from a $6.1M loss, though gross margin slipped to 27% from 30%. The company now expects FY2026 adjusted EBITDA of $62M–$72M, above analysts’ $61.1M forecast.
  • Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) +5% - Shares rose after the carrier delivered a stronger-than-expected Q3, with non-GAAP EPS of $1.71, beating estimates by $0.19, and adjusted revenue of $15.2B, up 4.2% Y/Y and $120M above consensus. For FY2025, Delta guided EPS of around $6, exceeding the $5.77 consensus, with free cash flow of $3.5B–$4B and gross leverage below 2.5x. In Q4, the airline expects revenue growth of 2%–4% vs. the 1.24% forecast, an operating margin of 10.5%–12%, and EPS of $1.60–$1.90, with a midpoint of $1.75, above the $1.64 consensus.

Biggest stock losers

  • Apogee Therapeutics (NASDAQ:APGE) -10% - Shares plunged after the company priced its public offering at $41 per share, raising approximately $300M in gross proceeds. The offering comprises 6.95M common shares and 365,853 pre-funded warrants, both priced at $41, with the warrants exercisable immediately. The deal, expected to close on October 10, 2025, also grants underwriters a 30-day option to buy up to 1.1M additional shares. Apogee said proceeds will support advancement of its clinical-stage biologics pipeline targeting inflammatory and immunology markets.
  • AZZ (NYSE:AZZ) -6% - Shares slid after the company missed FQ2 expectations and presented a mixed outlook for the full year. While AZZ maintained its FY2026 guidance, the midpoint for its projected sales of $1.675B came in slightly above the consensus of $1.66B. However, the midpoint for adjusted diluted EPS of $6.00 missed the analyst consensus of $6.04. The maintained guidance reflects expectations for anticipated market conditions, lower interest expense, and a 24% effective tax rate, while excluding any M&A activity or federal regulatory changes.
